title: Nonhomogeneous ODEs

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-03-calculus-linear-algebra.md

setup.narrative: Solve two nonhomogeneous ODEs that share the same homogeneous part: (i) y'' + y' + y = 1 and (ii) y'' + y' + y = x.
rules: The general solution is y = y_h + y_p, where y_h is the homogeneous solution and y_p is any particular solution.
The homogeneous part y'' + y' + y = 0 has the solution we derived in the previous problem: y_h = e^(-x/2) * (c1*cos(sqrt(3)*x/2) + c2*sin(sqrt(3)*x/2)).
When the right-hand side is a polynomial, try a polynomial of the SAME DEGREE for y_p.
setup.question: Find y(x) for both right-hand sides.

learningObjectives: Recognize that any particular solution will do (combined with the full homogeneous family).
Guess y_p as a polynomial of the same degree as the right-hand side.
Match coefficients to determine the polynomial's coefficients.

stages.label: Why the structure 'y_h + y_p' works
stages.prompt: Why is the general solution y = y_h + y_p?

options.text: Because the operator L[y] = y'' + y' + y is linear: L[y_h] = 0 and L[y_p] = d, so L[y_h + y_p] = 0 + d = d. Also, ANY two solutions differ by a homogeneous solution, so y_h covers ALL the freedom.
Only when the right-hand side is constant.

stages.explanation: Linearity: the solution space of the nonhomogeneous equation is an affine space -- pick any one point (y_p), then translate by the homogeneous space (y_h).

stages.label: Find y_p for y'' + y' + y = 1
stages.prompt: Try y_p = constant. What constant works?
stages.explanation: Constant guess: y_p = c. Then y_p' = y_p'' = 0, so y_p'' + y_p' + y_p = c. Setting c = 1 makes the equation hold.

stages.label: Find y_p for y'' + y' + y = x
stages.prompt: The right side is degree 1. Try y_p = m*x + n. Compute and match coefficients.

options.text: y_p'' + y_p' + y_p = 0 + m + (mx + n) = mx + (m + n) = x.  Match: m = 1 and m + n = 0, so n = -1.  y_p = x - 1.

stages.explanation: y_p = mx + n. y_p' = m. y_p'' = 0. Sum = mx + (m + n). Match coefficient-by-coefficient with x: x term gives m = 1; constant term gives m + n = 0, so n = -1. Particular solution: y_p = x - 1.

stages.label: Verify y_p = x - 1
stages.prompt: Check by plugging y_p = x - 1 into y'' + y' + y.

stages.label: Commit both general solutions
stages.prompt: Combine the homogeneous solution y_h = e^(-x/2) * (c1*cos(sqrt(3)*x/2) + c2*sin(sqrt(3)*x/2)) with each y_p.
stages.explanation: (i) y = e^(-x/2) * (c1*cos(sqrt(3)*x/2) + c2*sin(sqrt(3)*x/2)) + 1. (ii) y = e^(-x/2) * (c1*cos(sqrt(3)*x/2) + c2*sin(sqrt(3)*x/2)) + (x - 1). Two free constants in each (from the second-order homogeneous part).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Solve y'' + y' + y = e^x. (Try y_p = A * e^x and find A.)
What if the right-hand side were a HOMOGENEOUS solution, e.g., y'' + y' + y = e^(-x/2)*cos(sqrt(3)*x/2)? The standard guess would yield 0 = nonzero. How do you handle this RESONANCE?
Why is the principle 'general = homogeneous + particular' specific to LINEAR equations?
